title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Steel Components Pvt. Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal.
The respondent company failed to conduct a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of fourteen years and the alleged misconduct was not substantiated by any documentary or oral evidence.
The termination order was arbitrary and violative of Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution of India as it was passed with mala fide intent to suppress legitimate trade union activity.
The Labour Court's award was well-reasoned and did not warrant interference under Article 226 of the Constitution of India.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court dismissed the writ petition filed by Hindustan Steel Components Pvt. Ltd. challenging the Labour Court's award directing reinstatement of Ramesh Kumar Sharma with full back wages. The Court held that the termination was illegal as it was carried out without a proper domestic inquiry and in violation of natural justice. The petitioner-employee's reinstatement with continuity of service and 75% back wages was upheld.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 4471 of 2023

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... PETITIONER
VERSUS
HINDUSTAN STEEL COMPONENTS PVT. LTD. & ANR. ... RESPONDENTS

Date of Decision: 14th September, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARVIND PRASAD SINGH

JUDGMENT

1. This writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India by the petitioner-employee challenging the order dated 03.03.2023 passed by the Additional Labour Court, Delhi in ID Case No. 112/2018, whereby the learned Labour Court allowed the claim of the workman-respondent and directed his reinstatement with continuity of service and 75% back wages.

2. Brief facts as emerging from the record are that the respondent-workman, Ramesh Kumar Sharma, was employed as a Senior Machine Operator with the petitioner company since the year 2007. By an order dated 17.08.2017, his services were terminated with immediate effect on the ground of alleged insubordination and habitual absenteeism. The workman raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court by the appropriate Government.

3. The Labour Court, after appreciation of evidence led by both parties, returned a finding that the petitioner-company had failed to hold a domestic inquiry before passing the order of termination. It further held that the alleged charges were not proven and that the termination was tainted with victimization on account of the workman's active participation in trade union activities.

4. Learned counsel for the petitioner-company strenuously argued that the Labour Court fell into error in interfering with a purely managerial decision and that sufficient material existed to justify the termination. He relied upon the charge sheet dated 10.07.2017 to contend that the workman had been afforded an opportunity to respond.

5. This Court is unable to accept the said submission. A perusal of the charge sheet reveals that it was issued merely seven days before the termination order and no inquiry officer was ever appointed. No inquiry proceedings were conducted nor was any finding of guilt recorded. The right of an employee to be heard before being condemned is a fundamental facet of natural justice and cannot be dispensed with on the ground of expediency. This principle is well settled by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in a catena of judgments including M/s Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate, (2005) 2 SCC 489.

6. The finding of the Labour Court that the termination was motivated by anti-union animus is a finding of fact arrived at after appreciation of oral and documentary evidence. This Court, in exercise of its jurisdiction under Article 226, does not sit as a court of appeal over findings of fact recorded by the Labour Court unless such findings are perverse or based on no evidence. No such case has been made out here.

7. Regarding the relief of reinstatement with back wages, it is noticed that the workman was out of service for a period exceeding six years. He was aged approximately 42 years at the time of termination and there is no material on record to show that he secured alternative gainful employment during this period. The award of 75% back wages is just and equitable in the facts and circumstances of the present case.

8. This Court finds no infirmity, illegality or perversity in the impugned award dated 03.03.2023 passed by the Additional Labour Court, Delhi. The writ petition is accordingly dismissed with costs of Rs. 25,000/- to be paid by the petitioner-company to the workman within four weeks from today.

9. The petitioner-company is directed to reinstate the workman within six weeks from the date of this judgment and release the back wages within a period of eight weeks.

ARVIND PRASAD SINGH, J.
SEPTEMBER 14, 2023
